primary.hook: Are AI Agents Your Next Security Nightmare? (What Everyone’s Missing About ‘Autonomy’)
core_idea: Most teams still treat agents as fancy chatbots, not security principals with real permissions.
The main risk isn’t the base model – it’s the action space you expose and how you bind tools, data, and credentials.
There are repeatable anti-patterns: over-trusting tool output, unconstrained action spaces, brittle guardrails, and no separation between ‘thinking’ and ‘doing’.
You can borrow proven patterns from security engineering (least privilege, blast radius, capability tokens, auditability) and apply them to LLM agents before you ship them into production.
primary.draft_post:
[Slide 1]
Title: AI Agents Aren’t Smart Assistants. They’re New Users With Root Access.

Everyone is stress-testing models.
Almost no one is stress-testing what their agents are allowed to *do*.

If you give an LLM the ability to:
- call APIs
- write to your DB
- send emails / Slack
- touch cloud resources

…you didn’t just add “autonomy”.
You created a new, barely-understood security principal.

That’s where the real risk lives.

This carousel is about the mistakes I keep seeing in early agent systems – and the non-obvious patterns that actually reduce blast radius.

---
[Slide 2]
Title: Mistake #1 — Treating “Autonomy” as a UX Feature, Not a Security Boundary

How most teams reason:
“Let’s make the agent as hands-off as possible. Fewer human approvals = better UX.”

What they miss:
Every new tool you attach is a *new capability*.
Capabilities compose.

Read-only DB + file write + Slack send
= data exfiltration via Slack

The non-obvious pattern:
Design agents like you design privileged service accounts:
- Explicit threat model
- Explicit capabilities
- Explicit monitoring

If you wouldn’t give a junior engineer unrestricted prod access on day one, don’t give it to a v0 agent.

---
[Slide 3]
Title: Mistake #2 — Confusing Intent Alignment With Action Safety

“I added a system prompt with rules. The agent knows not to do bad things.”

This is model-centric thinking.

Reality:
- Prompts are *suggestions*, not enforcement.
- Prompt injection works alarmingly well.
- Tool call patterns get weird under distributional shift.

Non-obvious pattern:
Treat the LLM as *untrusted planning logic*.

The real guardrails live outside the model:
- Hard constraints on which tools can be called in which contexts
- Schema-level validation on every action
- Allowlists, not rely-on-the-prompt-lists

If your only safety layer is the system prompt, you don’t have a safety layer.

---
[Slide 4]
Title: Mistake #3 — Insecure Action Spaces (The Glue Is the Vulnerability)

Everyone audits:
- The model (evals, jailbreaks)
- The tools (auth, rate limits)

Almost no one audits:
- The *binding* between LLM and tools

Common anti-patterns I keep seeing:
- Natural-language tool descriptions that are overly broad
- Single catch-all “execute_code” tool with filesystem + network access
- DB tools that allow arbitrary SQL with prod credentials

Non-obvious pattern:
Your action space should be:
- Narrow: small, focused tools
- Typed: strict schemas, enums, and limits
- Contextual: tools only available in specific workflows / states

The more generic your tools, the more creative your failure modes.

---
[Slide 5]
Title: Mistake #4 — Over-Trusting Tool Output (Silent Compromise)

Most agent stacks do:
1) Model decides to call tool
2) Tool runs
3) Model trusts the tool output as ground truth

Two hidden problems:
- Tools can be compromised (e.g., prompt-injected retrieval, poisoned data)
- Tools can be misused (wrong params, wrong context)

Non-obvious pattern:
Sanity-check tools *and* their use:
- Cross-validate critical actions with a second tool or heuristic check
- Add cheap invariants (e.g., “rows changed < 1000” for DB writes)
- Log and diff state transitions: what exactly changed after each tool call?

Most scary incidents won’t look like a jailbreak.
They’ll look like a quiet series of “valid” tool calls.

---
[Slide 6]
Title: Mistake #5 — No Concept of Blast Radius for Agents

Teams often ask: “Can the agent do X?”
They rarely ask: “If this agent goes rogue, what’s the *worst* it could do?”

Patterns I’m seeing:
- Single agent identity with:
  - full prod DB access
  - ability to send external emails
  - access to internal documentation
- Same keys used in dev and prod

Non-obvious pattern:
Design agents with blast radius in mind:
- Environment separation (dev/stage/prod agents, different keys)
- Fine-grained scopes per workflow (read vs write, table-level, resource-level)
- Time-bounded capabilities (short-lived tokens, expiring sessions)

You’ll never get risk to zero.
You *can* make the worst case survivable.

---
[Slide 7]
Title: Mistake #6 — Mixing “Thinking” and “Doing” in One Agent

Typical design:
One agent that:
- plans
- decides
- executes

This is convenient.
It’s also hard to secure.

Why it’s a problem:
- No natural choke point to insert approvals
- No separation between reasoning errors and execution errors
- Hard to debug: you only see “the agent did something weird”

Non-obvious pattern:
Split your system:
- Planner agent: proposes a plan (natural language)
- Executor: a thin, highly-constrained layer that turns approved plans into actions

You’d never let a junior analyst both architect a migration and run it directly in prod.
Treat agents the same way.

---
[Slide 8]
Title: Mistake #7 — Assuming Logs = Security

“Yes, but we log everything the agent does.”

Logging is necessary.
It’s not sufficient.

Common issues:
- Logs are unstructured, impossible to query
- No defined alerts for dangerous patterns
- No replay/repro mechanism

Non-obvious pattern:
Design logs for:
- Forensics: full chain of thought → tools → state changes
- Diffing: before/after snapshots for critical resources
- Simulation: the ability to replay an interaction in a sandbox

If you only look at logs *after* an incident, you’re already late.

---
[Slide 9]
Title: What To Do Before You Ship Your First Agent to Production

Here’s a minimal checklist I now treat as non-negotiable:

1) Threat model the *capabilities*, not the model.
2) Enumerate tools and define:
   - exact scopes
   - rate limits
   - environments
3) Separate planning from execution.
4) Add invariants and sanity checks around tool use.
5) Implement least privilege and blast-radius limits.
6) Test agents in a sandbox with:
   - prompt injection
   - adversarial tool outputs

Most orgs don’t need fewer agents.
They need agents designed as security-aware systems, not clever demos.

If you’re building agents today, how are you constraining what they’re allowed to *do*?
primary.visual_suggestion: Clean, minimal design. Dark background, high-contrast text. Reusable visual motif: a simple “agent box” connected to tools, data, and external systems. Progression across slides visually tightens constraints around the agent box (e.g., broad red halo on early slides narrowing into defined, labeled boundaries on later ones). Use small callout diagrams for: (1) planning vs execution separation, (2) action space with narrow vs generic tools, (3) blast radius comparison (unbounded agent vs scoped workflows).
primary.why_this_works: It positions the creator as someone who has actually thought through and built agentic systems, not just played with chatbots. The post is contrarian in a precise way: it shifts attention from model-centric “safety” to capability-centric security, which is where real incidents will originate. Each slide calls out a concrete design mistake and pairs it with a specific, non-obvious pattern (untrusted planning logic, narrow action spaces, planner vs executor split, blast radius thinking). The language is practical and system-oriented, making it resonate with engineers, data scientists, and technical leaders who are trying to move from demos to production. It avoids hype and motivation, and instead offers a mental model: treat agents as new security principals with constrained capabilities, not as magic autonomous coworkers.

source_refs.title: Are AI Agents Your Next Security Nightmare?
Kaggle + Google’s Free 5-Day Gen AI Course

passed_checks: Explicitly focuses on what people get wrong about AI agents and security, not generic AI commentary.
Includes multiple non-obvious patterns (treat LLM as untrusted planner, design action spaces, blast radius thinking, planner/executor split).
Avoids motivational tone, hype language, and generic safety advice.
Stays grounded in realistic agent/tool orchestration scenarios and tradeoffs.
Carousel structure is clear: each slide is one mistake pattern + corrective pattern.
critic_notes: Could be further strengthened with one concrete real-world incident example (redacted) to make risks more visceral.
Some terms (blast radius, threat modeling) assume a moderately technical audience; if targeting a broader LinkedIn base, one or two could be briefly defined in-line.
The core framing is non-obvious and experience-driven: treating agents as security principals and the LLM as untrusted planning logic is not beginner-level or generic.

Each slide contains a specific mistake + corrective pattern with real tradeoffs (UX vs security, autonomy vs blast radius, generic tools vs narrow typed tools). This is the right level for practitioners moving from demos to production.
The content goes beyond model-centric safety tropes and focuses on action spaces, bindings, and capability design – this is where real systems thinking shows up.
Language is sharp, concrete, and free of hype/motivation fluff. It reads closer to an internal architecture review than a LinkedIn inspo post.

There are multiple non-trivial concepts that a shallow AI/beginner wouldn’t usually surface coherently in one piece: planner/executor split, capability composition, treating logs as forensics/simulation primitives, invariants on tool output, contextual tool availability, etc.

backups.title: Your RAG Isn’t Failing Because of the Model. It’s Failing Because of Your Retrieval Contracts.
backups.angle: Non-obvious pattern: most RAG failures are about brittle retrieval interfaces, schema drift, and missing invariants between search and generation, not embedding quality.
backups.hook: Everyone is swapping embeddings to fix their RAG. Almost no one is fixing the contract between retrieval and the LLM.
backups.why_now: RAG has become the default enterprise pattern, but most teams are in the “it works on our demo corpus” stage. This post would dissect specific retrieval/agent design mistakes (unbounded context windows, missing type guarantees, no feedback from generation back to retrieval) and show how to treat retrieval as a first-class API with stability guarantees, rather than a black-box vector search call.
backups.visual_suggestion: Side-by-side diagrams: naive RAG pipeline vs RAG with explicit contracts (typed queries, schemas, invariants). Show before/after examples of a query moving through the pipeline, highlighting where things break when contracts are missing.

backups.title: LLM Evaluations Are Lying to You (Because Your Tasks Aren’t What You Think They Are)
backups.angle: Mistake + insight: teams are over-indexing on leaderboard metrics or synthetic evals that don’t match their real workflows; the non-obvious pattern is to evaluate at the system-boundary task level, not the model-response level.
backups.hook: Your evals say your model is great. Your users say it’s useless. The problem isn’t the model – it’s what you’re actually evaluating.
backups.why_now: As more orgs move from POCs to production, they try to retrofit evals onto already-built systems. This is where misalignment between metrics and reality becomes painful. A post breaking down common eval anti-patterns (grading single turns, ignoring tool interactions, evaluating text instead of task completion) is very timely.
backups.visual_suggestion: Carousel panels that show a simple user task (e.g., “update this customer’s plan”) with two levels of evaluation: (1) naive: scoring the generated text, (2) system-aware: scoring state changes, tool calls, and user correction rate. Use flow diagrams with “where eval attaches” markers to show the shift in perspective.

generated_content.selected_topic_reason: This topic sits directly at the intersection of the creator’s experience (data/analytics, now building AI systems) and a rapidly emerging, under-explored risk area: agent security. It allows them to sound like a systems thinker who has actually wired up tools, data, and models, and has seen non-obvious failure modes up close. The angle is slightly contrarian (stop obsessing over the base model; start worrying about what your agents are allowed to do), and very well suited to a carousel format where each slide isolates a distinct mistake pattern and an actionable corrective design pattern.

signals.title: Tell HN: Fiverr left customer files public and searchable
signals.excerpt:
Fiverr (gig work/task platform, competitor to Upwork) uses a service called Cloudinary to process PDF/images in messaging, including work products from the worker to client.

Besides the PDF processing value add, Cloudinary effectively acts like S3 here, serving assets directly to the web client. Like S3, it has support for signed/expiring URLs. However, Fiverr opted to use public URLs, not signed ones, for sensitive client-worker communication.

Moreover, it seems like they may be serving public HTML somewhere that links to these files. As a result, hundreds are in Google search results, many containing PII.

Example query: site:fiverr-res.cloudinary.com form 1040

In fact, Fiverr actively buys Google Ads for keywords like "form 1234 filing" despite knowing that it does not adequately secure the resulting work product, causing the preparer to violate the GLBA/FTC Safeguards Rule.

Responsible Disclosure Note -- 40 days have passed since this was notified to the designated vulnerability email (security@fiverr.com). The security team did not reply. Therefore, this is being made public as it doesn't seem eligible for CVE/CERT processing as it is not really a code vulnerability, and I don't know anyone else who would care about it.

Comments URL: https://news.ycombinator.com/item?id=47769796

Points: 443

# Comments: 88
